channel.title: Notes on daniel klotz |
channel.description: Recent content in Notes on daniel klotz |

item.title: model checking
item.description: Looking Inside The Blackbox. This is blog-post/tutorial about the usage of backprop do obtain some qualitative understanding of neural-networks. Not to brad, but I had a similar idea in the context of “data-assimilation for Deep Learning based rainfall-runoff modeling”. As in the blogpost, the idea was to backprop with regard to the inputs (instead of the parameters), but with the goal to get (a) a better forecast and (b) obtain a qualitative understand about what the networks thinks about the world.

item.title: calm tech
item.description: Permacomputing As far as I can tell permacomputing is so far mainly a nice essay that lays out a vision by the amazing viznut. Here is how it starts: Over the last few hundred years of human civilization, there has been a dramatic increase in the consumption of artificially produced energy. In the overarching story, this is often equated with “progress”. In the computer world, this phenomenon gets multiplied by itself: “progress” facilitates ever greater densities of data storage and digital logic, thus dramatically exploding the availability of computing resources.

item.title: goblin notes
item.description: Experimental hollowness: When it becomes unclear if a benchmarking study, an empirical experiment or a simulation study contributes anything other than an improvement with regard to a under-specified metric (any perhaps even introduces with regard to some other relevant metrics). Empirical death end: When a theory does not admit any (more) experiments that can lead to its refutation or advancement. See: Sabine_Hossenfelder’s Lost in Math. Winner’s forgetfulness: Winning induces forgetfulness.

item.title: map & territory
item.description: Liu & Regier (2020): Flows Succeed Where GANs Fail: Lessons from Low-Dimensional Data. Abstract: Normalizing flows and generative adversarial networks (GANs) are both approaches to density estimation that use deep neural networks to transform samples from an uninformative prior distribution to an approximation of the data distribution. There is great interest in both for general-purpose statistical modeling, but the two approaches have seldom been compared to each other for modeling non-image data.

item.title: multi multi
item.description: Google AI: Optimizing Multiple Loss Functions with Loss-Conditional Training (paper) First paragraph: In many machine learning applications the performance of a model cannot be summarized by a single number, but instead relies on several qualities, some of which may even be mutually exclusive. For example, a learned image compression model should minimize the compressed image size while maximizing its quality. It is often not possible to simultaneously optimize all the values of interest, either because they are fundamentally in conflict, like the image quality and the compression ratio in the example above, or simply due to the limited model capacity.

item.title: movies
item.description: Regisseurs I watch: Quentin Tarantino, Stanley Kubrick, Hayao Miyazaki, Kar-Wai Wong, Edgar Wright, Woody Allen, Wes Anderson, Jim Jarmusch, Terry Gilliam. Short notes about movies: Paterson. A slow burner. Did not enjoy it much in cinema, but it became one of my favorite movies after watching it multiple times. Dead Man. A good movie. Just not my pair of shoes. Bill & Ted Face the Music. It is what it is.

item.title: books
item.description: Short notes on books: Taleb (2007): Fooled by Randomness. A gem. Page (2018) The Model Thinker. Very enjoyable. Proctor & Schiebinger (ed, 2008) Proctor, R: Agnotology. More interesting than I expected, less than I hoped. Ende (1994) Zettelkasten. Großartig! White et al. (1993) Complexity and Chaos. Not sure what to think about this thing. Definitely not my pair of shoes. Childers (2013) Philosophy and Probability. Very good book. Otto (2019) Wütendes Wetter.

item.title: paper extracts
item.description: Sunset Salvo In the following are some memorable quotes from John W. Tukey’s speech sunset salvo. My title is borrowed from Estil green who used it when he retired as Executive Vice-President at Bell Laboratories about two decades ago. He was an executive, so he really retired. +++ The simplicty of wrting thesis in, and assessing the correctess of, mathematical statistics […] will aways ensure expansion of that corner.

item.title: metrics 1
item.description: We only have sub-optimal metrics for reporting and evaluating model performance in rainfall-runoff modelling. This is not a controversial opinion (see for example Schaefi and Gupta, 2007 or Gupta and Kling, 2011). Many metrics implicitly assume some form of well behaved error distribution. In this series (?) I want to explore what happens if this is not the case by using simulation (using the NSE, the KGE and Pearson Correlation).

item.title: media diet
item.description: 30.Mai.2020 Very random one. Mostly some brain candy; some more, some less healthy. Dave Bull is a maker of japanes woodblock prints and intersting stories to tell. The muji fountain pen works great. I do not get how audible works. (comment: I do now & I dont like it. ) The Book Brood is a strange yet endearing youtube channel about … books and family life. The [green renaissance] makes captivating videos about individuals (e.

item.title: p5js: Particles1
item.description: Playing around with the new p5.js editor… // actual script --------------------------------------------------------------- function setup() { var canvas = createCanvas(720, 450); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); system = new ParticleSystem(createVector(width/2, 50)); } function draw() { background(20,30,50, 150); system.addParticle(); system.run(); } // A simple Particle class var Particle = function(position) { this.acceleration = createVector(-0.00015*(mouseX-0.5*width),-0.00012*(mouseY-0.5*height)); this.velocity = createVector(random(-0.5, 0.5), random(-1, 0)); this.old_position = position.copy(); this.position = position.copy(); this.lifespan = 255; }; Particle.prototype.run = function() { this.

item.title: if else pipes
item.description: A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here): library(magrittr) runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"} ## [1] "bar" Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used: library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE) runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", . > 0.5 ~ "bar") ## [1] "foo" A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

item.title: Plotly Test
item.description: A test for concatenating multiple plots in plotly (Adapted from ror2 ). It seems to have problems when zoomed in. ## date min QOBS_0001 QOSI_0001 QSIM_0001 QOBS_0002 QOSI_0002 ## 1 2007-01-01 0 2.98 3.48 3.48 2.56 3.11 ## 2 2007-01-01 0 2.89 3.48 3.48 2.56 3.11 ## 3 2007-01-01 0 2.64 3.48 3.48 2.57 3.11 ## 4 2007-01-01 0 2.51 3.48 3.48 2.57 3.

item.title: Feedback tribbles
item.description: The ordering and display of tables in Rmarkdown (aka data.frames or tibbles) has become so good. I actually prefer it over using print right now. Especially, because I can format in in an intuitive way by using the tribble() function. Here is an example. Let us say I want to summarize the basic set up for an optimization. Some dummy variables might look like this: library(tibble) number_iterations <- 1000 stoping_criterion <- -1 stepsize <- 0.

item.title: p5js: 3D Test
p5js: Function Plotting
item.description: A little test for plotting functions. // actual script --------------------------------------------------------------- var c_size = 450 var midpoint = 0.5*c_size; var resolution = 10; var x1 = 0; var y1 = 0; var grid_div = 15; function setup() { background("#fff"); var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); noLoop(); strokeWeight(1); stroke(50,50,50,150); line(midpoint,0,midpoint,c_size) line(0,midpoint,c_size,midpoint) // stroke(50,50,50,23); for (var i=0; i

item.title: p5js: Floating Sticks
item.description: Adapted from here // actual script --------------------------------------------------------------- var gl; var gl2; var tStep = 0.3; var all_time = 0.0 var c_size = 450; var midpoint = c_size/2 var first_click = false function setup() { background("#FFF"); var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); // Line /*gl = new GrowingSticks(false); gl.addPoint(10,0.5*height,true,20); for(var x=20; x1){ if(this.closed){ this.springs.pop(); } this.springs.push(new Spring(this.points[this.points.length-2],this.points[this.points.length-1],len)); if(this.closed){ this.springs.push(new Spring(this.points[this.points.length-1],this.points[0],len)); } } }, insertPoint: function(fixed=false, len0, len1, firstIndex){ if(this.

item.title: tidy evaluation 2
item.description: The first post is more explaatory and can be found here. This one is more a note-taking effort and hence much more be spartan. The main goal is to go through some of the functionality of the rlang package. Basics rlang has multiple constructor-functions that can be used instead of the base Rcombine operator c(). These are: lgl(...), int(...), dbl(...), cpl(...), chr(..., .encoding = NULL), bytes(...) and ll(...). The idea here is that this way the code is more explicit than using c() (The resulting data can be inferred from the functions).

item.title: p5js: linegrow
item.description: Adapted from here // actual script --------------------------------------------------------------- var gl; var tStep = 0.3; var c_size = 450 var midpoint = c_size/2 function setup() { var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); // Line /*gl = new GrowingLine(false); gl.addPoint(10,0.5*height,true,20); for(var x=20; x1){ if(this.closed){ this.springs.pop(); } this.springs.push(new Spring(this.points[this.points.length-2],this.points[this.points.length-1],len)); if(this.closed){ this.springs.push(new Spring(this.points[this.points.length-1],this.points[0],len)); } } }, insertPoint: function(fixed=false, len0, len1, firstIndex){ if(this.points.length 1){ let midPos = p5.Vector.add(this.points[firstIndex].pos,this.points[firstIndex+1].pos).mult(0.5); this.points.splice(firstIndex+1,0,new Point(midPos.x, midPos.y, fixed)); this.

item.title: p5js: Doodle Flower
item.description: A little doodle with p5.js Code: var c_size = 450 var midpoint = 0.5*c_size; var c_pad = c_size - 20 var alpha_decay = 20 var easing = 0.03; var x = midpoint var y = midpoint var red = 30 var green = 144 var blue = 255 var update_time = 0 var update_step = 100 function setup() { background("#EBEBEB"); var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); } function mousePressed() { red = 30 green = 144 blue = 255 } function draw() { if (frameCount > update_time) { update_time += update_step red = round( random(1,244) ) green = round( random(1,244) ) blue = round( random(1,244) ) } //square stroke("#EBEBEB") fill("#EBEBEB") rect(0,0,c_size-1,c_size-1); // outer circles fill("#fff"); stroke("#fff") ellipse(0,0,c_size,c_size); ellipse(0,c_size,c_size,c_size); ellipse(c_size,0,c_size,c_size); ellipse(c_size,c_size,c_size,c_size); // line stroke(red, green, blue); line(midpoint, midpoint, x, y) // cicrles 1 fill(red, green, blue); stroke(red, green, blue); ellipse(midpoint, midpoint, c_pad/6, c_pad/6); ellipse(x, y, 25, 25); ellipse(0.

item.title: p5js: flares
item.description: Dawdling flares with p5.js. Code: var canvas_size = 450; var canvas_pad = 50; var radius = (canvas_size-canvas_pad)/2; var midpoint = canvas_size/2; var ball_radius = 150; // Blob def function Blob(x,y) { this.pos = createVector(x,y) this.vel = p5.Vector.random2D(); this.vel.mult(10,20) this.r = ball_radius // this.update= function() { this.pos.add(this.vel) var pos_out = (midpoint-this.pos.x)**2 + (midpoint-this.pos.y)**2 >= (radius-0.3*ball_radius)**2 if (pos_out) { this.vel.x *= -1 this.vel.y *= -1 } } } // script for running metaballs var b=[] function setup() { //b = new Blob(100,100) var canvas = createCanvas(canvas_size, canvas_size); canvas.

item.title: p5js: star
item.description: Another little Javascript-animation based on Gaussian ellipses. Code: var c_size = 450 var half_c_size = 0.5*c_size; var c_pad = c_size - 20 var time = 0.0 var increment = 0.002; var time_to_update = time + 2*increment; var increment_to_update = 0.03; var n = 0 var m = 0 function setup() { background("#DDDCD3"); var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size); canvas.parent('sketch-holder'); // info: textSize(19); stroke(221,220,211); fill(221,220,211); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/4, c_pad/4); // fill("#EEEDE4"); text(" ~68% \n of the radii", c_pad/2-40, c_size/2); // decoration: // outer circles fill(221,220,211,0); stroke(221,220,211); strokeWeight(3); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/3, c_pad/3); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/2, c_pad/2); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad, c_pad); // outer ellipses strokeWeight(1); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/3, c_pad/4); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/4, c_pad/3); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/2, c_pad/3); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/3, c_pad/2); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad , c_pad/2); ellipse(half_c_size, half_c_size, c_pad/2, c_pad ); } function draw() { translate(width/2, height/2); rotate(frameCount / 50.

item.title: p5js: anim
Rainy Meanderings
item.description: Here I will collect notes and references about the conceptualization in hydrology. Klemes (Conceptualization and scale in hydrology, 1986; https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-1694(83)90208-1) provides the basics. The abstract reads: It is argued that the levels of scale at which a meaningful conceptualization of physical processes is possible are not arbitrary and their range is not continuous. Formulations appropriate at a given level usually are not applicable at the immediately adjoining levels. This is seen as one of the important reasons for the slow progress of hydrological science on basin scale since it is dominated by different effects than those observed at the immediately lower level of channel reaches and hillslopes which represent the focus of current hydrological research.

item.title: Visualizing Uncertainty
item.description: I am not sure where one should start with this topic. Visualizing uncertainty is hard. in general, it is still an unsolved problem. Back in the days even the mighty Nathan Yau wrote: I still struggle with uncertainty and visualization. I haven’t seen many worthwhile solutions other than the old standbys, boxplots and histograms, which show distributions. But how many people understand spread, skew, etc? It’s a small proportion, which poses an interesting challenge.

item.title: Intro
item.description: Currently everything is very scrappy here and it will probably remain so for quite some time. I will try to put some pretty random notes on random things on here. The idea is that each “blogpost” will be expanded over time and is thus not finished. I may provide some kind of update news as the project goes along; that will however depend on how everything plays out. Some Definitions and Concepts scrap (/skɹæp/):
